text: The surgical team sets up ports for the right upper lobectomy, starting with the eight millimeter camera port placed blindly at the junction of the sternum and xiphoid. The anterior and posterior twelve millimeter ports are positioned for stapling, with the posterior port crucial for accessing the right upper lobe vein and the first branch of the pulmonary artery. The eight millimeter fourth arm is positioned near the spine in the seventh intercostal space, while the twelve millimeter assistant port is triangulated between the camera port and medial stapling arm, ensuring adequate space for the assistant.

The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to apply pressure on the superior segment of the right lower lobe while dissecting the posterior hilum. The posterior mediastinal pleura is divided up to the bronchus intermedius, revealing subcarinal lymph nodes. The vagus nerve is visible over the esophagus, and a subcarinal lymph node is extracted. The surgical field shows minor bleeding in the vascular space during the dissection.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery at the highest setting to dissect the posterior side of the bronchus intermedius and the right upper lobe bronchus. The dissection focuses on the space between these bronchi, targeting the station XI sump lymph node. The node is carefully dissected out, avoiding the superior segmental artery and posterior segmental vein, which are located behind it. The junction between the right upper lobe bronchus and bronchus intermedius is visible, along with the superior segmental vein. The sump lymph node is extracted to improve exposure to the hilum from the posterior aspect.

The surgeon performs a bloody dissection, reflecting the lung towards the feet and using a zero-degree camera to view the hilum. The dissection focuses on the station 2R and 4R lymph nodes, removing them in a packet with surrounding fat. Key anatomical landmarks include the superior vena cava medially, the trachea laterally, and the azygos vein inferiorly, with the pericardium as the deep margin. The area is packed with Surgicel, and the surgeon notes the hypervascularity of the tissue. The dissection reveals the first branch of the pulmonary artery, the right upper lobe bronchus, and potentially the right upper lobe vein.

The surgeon uses a navigation bronchoscopy to mark the middle lobe tumor with ICG dye and methylene blue, ensuring optical visibility for resection. The lung is reflected posteriorly to expose the superior pulmonary vein and identify the junction between the right upper lobe vein and the right middle lobe vein. The interlobar pulmonary artery is visible behind the superior pulmonary vein. The surgeon carefully dissects around these vascular structures using bipolar cautery, noting the absence of thermal spread.

The surgeon marks the pulmonary artery with a vessel loop for easy identification and division. They prefer to divide arterial structures before the vein to minimize bleeding and vascular congestion. The surgeon carefully dissects the thick fissure to expose the pulmonary artery, avoiding excessive 'fissure diving' to prevent air leaks. Using bipolar cautery, they create a tunnel along the pulmonary artery, sweeping away loose connective tissue to open the posterior side of the major fissure. The yellow hue of the pulmonary artery is visible, with a small branch vessel noted.

The surgeon uses a bipolar cautery to dissect through the posterior hilar space, previously cleared of the station eleven sump lymph node, and creates a tunnel above the pulmonary artery. A Robnell PVC catheter is placed in the plane above the artery, and a robotic stapler with a white load is used to divide the posterior fissure. The interlobar pulmonary artery is exposed, and a small branch of the pulmonary artery is carefully dissected and double-clipped with metal clips to prevent bleeding.

The surgeon uses hot scissors to open the space around the posterior ascending branch of the pulmonary artery, which is visible entering the posterior segment. Sponge rolls are used for blunt dissection and to manage any bleeding. A red rubber catheter is placed behind the artery, and a gray load stapler is used to divide the artery. The right upper lobe bronchus is exposed, with peribronchial lymph nodes visible. The surgeon carefully dissects around the bronchus using a tip-up instrument, ensuring a safe approach with the pulmonary artery on the opposite side.

The surgeon uses a green load stapler to divide the right upper lobe bronchus, exposing the truncus anterior from the backside. The truncus anterior is bifurcated, with a few lymph nodes between it and the bronchus. The surgeon isolates the truncus anterior, noting the upper lobe vein is yet to be divided. The vein is marked with a vessel loop, and the red rubber catheter is positioned behind it. The surgeon prepares to staple the vein from the medial port, ensuring no injury to the truncus anterior. The pulmonary artery is visible behind the vein, and the vascular structures are divided. The bronchus is divided, and the surgeon notes the ICG marking for the middle lobe tumor, aiming to avoid parenchymal injury.

The surgeon repairs the incomplete minor fissure, using a blue load stapler to divide the lung parenchyma while ensuring the ICG dye marking the tumor is above the staple line. The connective tissue between the pulmonary artery and the lobe is dissected, and the fissure is completed by staying on top of the pulmonary artery. A green load stapler is used as the parenchyma thickens, with a red rubber catheter defining the safe plane above the artery. The procedure involves an extended right upper lobectomy, including a portion of the middle lobe, with both tumors identified as adenocarcinomas and completely resected.

The surgeon completes an extended right upper lobectomy, including part of the middle lobe, leaving a significant portion of the middle lobe intact. The tumor is placed in an anchor bag for removal. The procedure is described as a fissureless right upper lobectomy, with ICG used to mark the second tumor.
